title: Anthropic Launches Claude Opus 4.8 with Dynamic Workflows and Effort Control
summary: Anthropic released Claude Opus 4.8, its most capable flagship model yet, with improvements in agentic reasoning, honesty, and coding — plus new dynamic workflows that can run hundreds of parallel subagents in a single session.
content: Anthropic launched Claude Opus 4.8 on May 28, 2026, upgrading its flagship model with improvements across coding, agentic performance, reasoning, and honesty benchmarks at the same price as Opus 4.7. The model is notably 4× less likely to let code flaws pass unremarked, and on the Super-Agent benchmark it is the only model to complete every case end-to-end, while scoring 84% on Online-Mind2Web — beating both Opus 4.7 and GPT-5.5. Alongside the model, Anthropic introduced dynamic workflows for Claude Code (available in research preview), enabling hundreds of parallel subagents in a single session for codebase-scale migrations across hundreds of thousands of lines of code. Users on claude.ai also gain a new effort control that trades depth for speed, and fast mode for Opus 4.8 is now 3× cheaper than on previous models. Anthropic additionally previewed Project Glasswing and Claude Mythos Preview, a higher-intelligence model class currently in limited use for cybersecurity work, with broader release expected within weeks.

title: Illinois Passes Landmark AI Safety Bill Requiring Third-Party Audits of Frontier Labs
summary: Illinois became the first U.S. state to require independent third-party audits of frontier AI companies' safety practices after the state House passed SB 315 unanimously 110-0.
content: The Illinois House passed SB 315 on May 27, 2026, by a unanimous 110-0 vote, creating the first U.S. state law to mandate independent third-party audits of frontier AI companies including OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google DeepMind. The bill goes beyond existing disclosure laws in California and New York by requiring annual external safety audits and adding whistleblower protections for AI company employees. Governor JB Pritzker signaled he would sign the legislation, writing that 'Illinois is leading the nation in holding Big Tech accountable.' OpenAI and Anthropic both publicly endorsed the bill, while the Chamber of Progress — a trade group backed by Google, Amazon, and Andreessen Horowitz — opposed it, arguing companies would be forced to expose sensitive systems to untested auditors. The bill arrives days after President Trump reversed course on signing a federal executive order for voluntary AI safety testing, leaving states to fill the regulatory void as federal progress on AI legislation remains stalled.

title: OpenAI AI Model Solves 80-Year-Old Erdős Math Conjecture Autonomously
summary: An OpenAI general-purpose AI model autonomously disproved a famous 1946 conjecture by mathematician Paul Erdős, marking the first major open math problem solved by AI with minimal human intervention.
content: OpenAI revealed that one of its general-purpose AI models found a counterexample to the planar unit distance problem — a conjecture posed by Hungarian mathematician Paul Erdős in 1946 — using tools from algebraic number theory to show that point arrangements with far more unit-distance pairs than a square grid exist for infinitely many values of n. Fields Medallist Timothy Gowers wrote he would have recommended the paper for publication in the Annals of Mathematics 'without any hesitation,' calling it the most sophisticated AI-generated proof to date and the first AI result he finds 'interesting in itself.' The breakthrough required minimal human intervention beyond the initial prompt, with the full chain of thought published alongside the paper. Days later, Google DeepMind's AI resolved nine additional lesser open problems from Erdős's catalogue, signaling a broader wave of AI-driven mathematical discovery. Researchers note that while AI excels at encyclopedic mathematical knowledge and exhaustively exploring speculative search spaces, whether it can generate genuine conceptual leaps — the lightbulb moments of mathematics — remains an open question.

title: The Great AI Cost Panic: Enterprises Hit Token Budget Walls as Agent Spending Spirals
summary: Companies including Uber and Microsoft are reportedly dropping AI agent contracts after burning through annual token budgets in months, triggering a broader debate about whether agentic AI's productivity returns justify its soaring costs.
content: As agentic AI adoption surged in early 2026, companies like Uber and Microsoft are reportedly discontinuing Claude Code licenses after blowing through annual token budgets in a matter of months, with one consultant telling Axios that a client spent half a billion dollars in a single month on Claude alone. Data from Ramp shows the average business now spends 13× more on AI tokens than in January 2025, driven by agents that consume an average of 96,000 tokens per job — more text than the entire novel 'The Great Gatsby' — before generating an answer. Firms that created internal 'tokenmaxxing' leaderboards to maximize AI usage found that code churn (lines deleted vs. added) increased by more than 800% under high AI adoption without corresponding productivity gains, according to engineering analytics firm Faros AI. JPMorgan published a note titled 'AI Token Costs are Eating Internet Profits Alive,' while AI skeptics argue the spending backlash signals a bubble correction. Analysts counter that this recalibration is a normal phase of technology adoption — the question defining the AI industry in 2026 is whether the productivity bill will ultimately prove worth it.

title: xAI Launches Grok Build Beta, a Coding Agent to Rival Claude Code and OpenAI Codex
summary: Elon Musk's xAI released Grok Build, a command-line coding agent for professional developers featuring parallel subagents, MCP server support, and headless pipeline mode — directly challenging Anthropic's Claude Code and OpenAI's Codex.
content: xAI launched Grok Build in beta, a command-line coding agent designed for professional software engineers, now available to all SuperGrok and X Premium+ subscribers and placing it in direct competition with OpenAI's Codex and Anthropic's Claude Code. The tool allows developers to hand off complex coding tasks to an AI agent that plans, edits, and reviews changes autonomously, with a standout feature being parallel subagents — a single prompt about a performance regression can trigger twenty simultaneous worker agents investigating database queries, cache hit rates, deployments, and endpoint performance concurrently. Grok Build integrates seamlessly with existing developer setups, supporting AGENTS.md files, MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers, plugins, hooks, and skills out of the box, so teams with established conventions need not reconfigure their environments. A headless mode activated with the -p flag enables the agent to run inside scripts and automated pipelines, and full ACP (Agent Communication Protocol) support allows developers to build their own orchestration systems on top of it. xAI has been candid that this is an early beta and is actively soliciting feedback through a built-in /feedback command.

title: Google Launches Gemini Omni: Multimodal AI Video Generator with Avatar Cloning and Physics Modeling
summary: Google released Gemini Omni, a multimodal AI video generation tool that accepts text, image, audio, and video inputs, featuring realistic avatar cloning and physics-based motion — its direct answer to OpenAI's Sora.
content: Google released Gemini Omni, a multimodal AI video generation tool it positions as doing 'for video what Nano Banana did for images,' accepting text, images, audio, and video as inputs and targeting both consumer and enterprise creators. The first tier, Gemini Omni Flash, includes an Avatars feature that creates digital replicas of users — generating videos that 'look and sound like you' — and incorporates physics modeling for realistic motion involving gravity, kinetic energy, and fluid dynamics, addressing a key weakness of earlier AI video tools that treated objects like ragdolls. The tool is rolling out to enterprise customers through the Gemini app, Google Flow, and YouTube Shorts, with SynthID digital fingerprinting embedded in all outputs to label AI-generated content. Google plans to expand Omni's capabilities beyond video generation, though it has not yet disclosed specifications around resolution, format support, or professional workflow integration. The avatar cloning feature introduces significant trust and verification challenges for video content, even with SynthID watermarking, as Gemini Omni enters direct competition with OpenAI's Sora.
